rationale: The citing paper uses Hoeffler (2003) repeatedly to frame its problem space: highly novel products are hard to understand, and mental simulation can improve acceptance when categorization is difficult. This prior stream is used to contrast the authors' focus on a different source of incongruity (the "what of it" benefit question), and to position their contribution as adding a new mechanism (cognitive flexibility) for improving evaluations. Because the citation helps establish conceptual background and research gap, it plays a meaningful framing role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The 2012 paper treats Hoeffler (2003) as part of the core prior literature on really new products, especially the role of mental simulation in improving evaluation. It builds from that foundation to propose and test cognitive flexibility as a broader route to resolving extreme incongruity.
